items.q: Why is negative feedback the default mechanism in homeostasis?
items.correct: It opposes deviations from a set point, returning the variable toward its normal value — keeping internal conditions stable.
items.misconception: Negative feedback amplifies changes to make them more noticeable.
items.random_wrong: Negative feedback only operates when something goes wrong in the body.
items.why: The word 'negative' confuses students into thinking it amplifies/worsens, but in control theory 'negative' just means 'opposes the change'.

items.q: How does sweating help cool you down?
items.correct: Evaporation of sweat absorbs energy (latent heat) from the skin, lowering body temperature.
items.misconception: Sweat is cold and directly cools the skin on contact.
items.random_wrong: Sweating removes excess heat by carrying it out as liquid waste.
items.why: Students don't connect sweating to evaporation; they think the liquid itself is the cooling agent, not the phase change.

items.q: What reads the mRNA codons during translation?
items.correct: tRNA molecules carrying specific amino acids — their anticodons pair with mRNA codons at the ribosome.
items.misconception: DNA directly reads the codons on mRNA and adds amino acids.
items.random_wrong: Ribosomes synthesise amino acids from scratch using the mRNA as a template.
items.why: Students miss the role of tRNA as the adaptor; they think mRNA is read by a direct chemical process rather than via tRNA matching.

items.q: How many possible chromosome combinations from independent assortment in human gametes?
items.why: Correct answer: 2^23. “2^22” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
It's Prophase I of meiosis. “Metaphase II” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
Exact answer: 46 chromosomes. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
